8 UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT 
9 SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF CALIFORNIA 
10 
11 BRIAN LUNDSTROM, Case No.: 18cv2856-GPC (MSB) 

12 Plaintiff, 
 DISCOVERY ORDER 
13 v. [ECF. NO. 202] 
14 CARLA YOUNG, et al., 
15 Defendants. 
16 
17 Pending before the Court is a Joint Discovery Motion, filed by Plaintiff Brian 
18 Lundstrom ("Plaintiff"), and Defendants Ligand Pharmaceuticals Incorporated ("Ligand") 
19 and Ligand Pharmaceuticals, Inc. 401(k) Plan (the "401(k) Plan") (collectively 
20 "Defendants") ("the Parties"), which asks this Court to determine whether certain 
21 communications between Ligand employees and outside counsel are protected from 
22 discovery by Plaintiff based on the attorney-client privilege. (ECF No. 202.) Specifically, 
23 Plaintiff seeks to compel Ligand's Chief People Officer, Audrey Warfield-Graham; 
24 Ligand's person most knowledgeable ("PMK"), Matt Korenberg; and Ligand's General 
25 Counsel, Charles Berkman, to answer questions regarding Berkman and Warfield-
26 Graham's communications with outside counsel at Latham & Watkins LLP ("Latham & 
27 Watkins") related to the Qualified Domestic Relations Order ("QDRO"), whose execution 
2 After carefully reviewing the Parties' Joint Discovery Motion, supporting exhibits, 
3 and the authorities cited therein, the Court finds this motion suitable for ruling on the 
4 pleadings presently before the Court. The Court finds that Ligand's communications 
5 with outside counsel at Latham & Watkins were directed to Defendants' desire to avoid 
6 civil liability in the face of competing demands from Plaintiff, Plaintiff's ex-wife Carla 
7 Young, and the court that issued the QDRO, and they are therefore protected by the 
8 attorney-client privilege from discovery by Plaintiff. 
9 I. FACTUAL BACKGROUND1 
10 Plaintiff's contentious divorce, finalized in Texas on July 30, 2014, and subsequent 
11 child support proceedings provide the backdrop for the issues in this dispute. (ECF No. 
12 202; ECF No. 92 at 5.) Plaintiff began working for Ligand approximately a year and a half 
13 after his divorce and started participating in the Ligand 401(k) Plan on or about April 1, 
14 2016. (ECF No. 92 at 5-6.) In July of 2017, Plaintiff's ex-wife, Carla Young, subpoenaed 
15 Ligand to produce documents related to Plaintiff's assets and income, including his 
16 Ligand 401(k) Plan. (ECF No. 202-2; ECF No. 202-11 at 2-3.) At Plaintiff's request, 
17 Ligand objected, and on August 2, 2017, Young sued Ligand in San Diego Superior Court 
18 to enforce the subpoena. (ECF No. 202 at 15; ECF No. 202-3.) 
19 Ligand also received court orders to withhold child support payments from 
20 Plaintiff's paychecks. (ECF No. 202 at 15.) Plaintiff requested that Ligand ignore or 
21 cease compliance with the orders on multiple occasions. (Id.; ECF No. 202-11 at 3.) In 
22 an email exchange with Ligand's Payroll and Accounts Payable Supervisor Debbie 
23 Schneider in October of 2017, Plaintiff directed Schneider to stop the child support 
24 withholdings once a certain amount had been paid. (ECF No. 202-4 at 3.) Schneider 
25 

26 
 1 The Parties' Joint Discovery Motion includes a "Joint Factual Background" section that evidently 
27 includes the Parties' agreed-upon facts. (ECF No. 202 at 15-17.) For efficiency's sake, the Court will 
2 withhold until we get a release." (Id.) 
3 Ligand's General Counsel, Charles Berkman, sought advice from Ligand's 
4 longstanding outside counsel John (Jake) Ryan, a litigation and trial partner at Latham & 
5 Watkins, related to Ligand's potential liability in the face of Plaintiff's numerous 
6 demands and threats about what Ligand should do in response to the subpoenas and 
7 child support orders. (ECF 202-10 at 2-3; ECF No. 202-11 at 3.) Ryan has served as 
8 Ligand's litigation counsel since 2007 and communicates with Berkman when "Ligand 
9 requires assistance with anticipated or ongoing litigation matters." (ECF No. 202-11 at 
10 2.) Specifically, Ryan represented Ligand in the 2017 subpoena litigation with Young and 
11 spoke multiple times with Berkman regarding the subpoenas and Texas child support 
12 orders. (Id. at 3.) 
13 On December 14, 2017, Young's attorney served Ligand with a QDRO issued by a 
14 Texas court on November 21, 2017. (ECF No. 202-5; ECF No. 202-11 at 3.) Ligand 
15 notified Plaintiff of the QDRO and provided him a copy by January 4, 2018. (ECF No. 202 
16 at 15.) Plaintiff emailed Warfield-Graham (chief people officer), Berkman, and Ligand 
17 President Matt Foehr on January 5, 2018, outlining five matters related to the QDRO he 
18 wanted Ligand to discuss with Fidelity's QDRO or legal department: 1) QDROs were 
19 supposed to be for marital assets, not post-marital assets, 2) the QDRO did not provide 
20 "exact dates" of when the 401(k) was earned, 3) the QDRO was silent as to whether it 
21 was issued for child support, alimony or marital property purposes, 4) that 401(k)s 
22 might be immune from transfer for employees of a certain age, and 5) the QDRO might 
23 not have resulted from a "real court hearing." (ECF No. 202-6 at 3.) Plaintiff suggested 
24 that Ligand acknowledge receipt of the QDRO but refrain from complying until Ligand 
25 performed some "necessary research," and discussed next steps with Plaintiff. (Id.) 
26 Foehr responded to Plaintiff the same day, reiterating via email what he had 
27 earlier expressed to Plaintiff in a phone call: "from a legal and corporate perspective, 
2 to do nothing "until [he had] a solid answer back." (Id.) Berkman replied that Plaintiff's 
3 areas of concern were not "likely to change how [he viewed the QDRO] unless [Plaintiff 
4 found] a way to challenge the court order and get it set aside, reversed, quashed, etc." 
5 (Id. at 2.) Later that afternoon, Plaintiff confirmed he had spoken to his "QDRO expert" 
6 and learned that it was common for plan administrators to take sixty days to respond. 
7 (Id.) 
8 On January 8, 2018, Plaintiff forwarded an email from his "QDRO expert," 
9 attorney Tony Jarrett, to Ligand and claimed the expert had identified major problems 
10 with the QDRO's legality. (ECF No. 202-7 at 3-4.) Plaintiff indicated he planned to 
11 follow the advice of Ligand and Jarrett and hire a lawyer to quash the QDRO. (Id. at 3.) 
12 On January 18, 2018, Warfield-Graham emailed Plaintiff stating outside legal 
13 counsel had "confirmed the QDRO satisfies all legal requirements" and Ligand would 
14 comply with the QDRO on February 1, 2018, unless Plaintiff obtained a stay of the 
15 QDRO. (ECF No. 202-7 at 2-3.) Again, Plaintiff responded with several demands of 
16 Ligand, including, "at a minimum, the company needs to make sure the 401(k) is only 
17 released against proper offset in child support arrearage payoff with the disbursement 
18 unit," and "Ligand must avoid double- and triple-dipping." (Id. at 2.) Plaintiff 
19 unsuccessfully appealed the QDRO to the Texas state court on January 26, 2018. (ECF 
20 No. 202 at 16.) The appellate court denied Plaintiff's subsequent writ of mandamus on 
21 February 5, 2018, at which point Ligand informed Plaintiff that it would begin the 
22 distribution process to comply with the QDRO. (Id.) After the Texas Supreme Court 
23 rejected the last of Plaintiff's challenges to the QDRO on November 16, 2018, Plaintiff 
24 filed suit against Ligand on December 20, 2018. (Id.; ECF No. 1.) 
25 / / / 
26 / / / 
27 / / / 
2 During years of litigation on the pleadings, the District Court dismissed Plaintiff's 
3 claims that either challenged the validity of the QDRO or relied on arguments 
4 concerning the QDRO's validity under the Rooker-Feldman and collateral estoppel 
5 doctrines. (ECF No. 64 at 14-15; ECF No. 120 at 16, 21.) Young is no longer a defendant 
6 and only three causes of action remain against Ligand and the 401(k) Plan: (1) Ligand 
7 distributed the benefits in Plaintiff's 401(k) account in violation of the 401(k) Plan's 
8 terms, (ECF No. 120 at 27); (2) Ligand violated section 1056(d)(3)(G)(i) of ERISA by failing 
9 to promptly provide Plaintiff a copy of the 401(k) Plan's procedures for determining the 
10 qualified status of a Domestic Relations Order, by not having a written policy in place 
11 outlining those procedures, and failing to send Plaintiff a written notification that the 
12 QDRO had satisfied the requirements under the 401(k) Plan and the Internal Revenue 
13 Code, (ECF No. 120 at 30); and (3) Ligand violated ERISA's anti-retaliation provision in 
14 section 510 by terminating Plaintiff in retaliation for exercising his rights under ERISA, 
15 (ECF No. 120 at 33). 
16 After holding an Early Neutral Evaluation and Case Management Conference, the 
17 Court issued a Scheduling Order on December 20, 2022. (ECF No. 136.) Under this 
18 order, fact discovery closed on July 21, 2023. (Id. at 2.) The Court granted Plaintiff's 
19 unopposed motions to extend the deadline for certain depositions, so long as those of 
20 Korenberg and Warfield-Graham were completed by August 31, 2023, and Berkman's 
21 deposition was taken by September 21, 2023. (ECF Nos. 184, 191.) Plaintiff took 
22 Warfield-Graham's deposition on July 31, 2023. (ECF No. 202-1.) Plaintiff now seeks to 
23 compel Warfield-Graham, Korenberg, and Berkman, to answer questions Ligand's 
24 counsel instructed them not to answer based on a claim of attorney-client privilege 
25 during previous depositions. (ECF No. 202 at 18.) Specifically, Plaintiff seeks to compel 
26 testimony directly related to email communications between Warfield-Graham and 
27 Latham & Watkins attorneys Holly Bauer, Jake Ryan, Sandhya Chandrasekhar, and 
2 2018, and January 27, 2018. (ECF No. 202 at 18; ECF No. 202-9.) Ligand maintains these 
3 emails are protected by the attorney-client privilege.2 (ECF No. 202 at 23.) 
4 III. DISCUSSION 
5 Plaintiff claims that the email communications at-issue relate to Ligand's 
6 administration of his 401(k) plan with respect to the QDRO before a final determination 
7 of the QDRO's validity had been made, so he is entitled to their disclosure under the 
8 fiduciary exception to attorney-client privilege. (ECF No. 202 at 19.) Additionally, 
9 Plaintiff claims that because a final determination had not yet been made about the 
10 QDRO's validity, he—not his ex-wife—was the true beneficiary of the Plan's 401(k) at 
11 the time of the communications, so he is entitled to their disclosure. (Id. at 22.) Lastly, 
12 Plaintiff claims that because he had not threatened Ligand with "imminent litigation" at 
13 the time the communications took place Ligand cannot assert attorney-client privilege 
14 over the contents. (Id. at 20.) 
15 Defendant Ligand argues that the communications were made with outside 
16 counsel for Ligand's own benefit after Ligand recognized that if it "ignored the QDRO, it 
17 would imminently face a second litigation from Young," but "[i]f it complied, it would 
18 imminently face litigation from Plaintiff (who sued a month after the Texas courts 
19 rejected his QDRO appeal)." (Id. at 24.) As such, the advice was given in anticipation of 
20 litigation and was note related to plan administration, so the communications are 
21 protected by the attorney-client privilege and not subject to the fiduciary exception. 
22 (Id.) Ligand further claims it had already determined the QDRO was valid on January 5, 
23 2018, before the communications at-issue took place. (ECF No. 202 at 25.) 
24 

25 
26 2 Though Defendants' position statement asserts that Plaintiff seeks communications protected by 
 both attorney-client privilege and work product doctrine, all of Defendants' briefing is targeted to the 
27 attorney-client privilege and the fiduciary exception thereto. (See ECF No. 202 at 23-27.) For this 
2 and Plaintiff lacks any right to invoke the fiduciary exception. (ECF No. 202 at 27.) 
3 A. Legal Standard 
4 Attorney-client privilege protects "confidential disclosures by a client to an 
5 attorney made in order to obtain legal assistance." Fisher v. United States, 425 U.S. 391, 
6 403 (1976). The privilege recognizes that a client's freedom to communicate openly and 
7 honestly with an attorney without fear of disclosure enables a fully informed attorney to 
8 provide sound legal advice to the client. Upjohn Co. v. United States, 449 U.S. 383, 389 
9 (1981). Preserving the privilege is "essential to the just and orderly operation of our 
10 legal system." United States v. Bauer, 132 F.3d 504, 510 (9th Cir. 1997). "A party 
11 asserting the attorney-client privilege has the burden of establishing the 
12 relationship and the privileged nature of the communication." United States v. Ruehle, 
13 583 F.3d 600, 607 (9th Cir. 2009) (quoting Bauer, 132 F.3d at 507). 
14 Relevant here, the Ninth Circuit is one of several that recognizes a fiduciary 
15 exception to the attorney-client privilege in certain situations. United States v. Mett, 
16 178 F.3d 1058, 1062-1063 (9th Cir. 1999). Specifically, in the context of ERISA, an 
17 employer acting as an ERISA fiduciary may not assert attorney-client privilege against a 
18 plan's beneficiaries on matters of plan administration. Id. at 1063 (citing Becher v. Long 
19 Is. Lighting Co., 129 F.3d 268, 272 (2d Cir. 1997)). Two alternative rationales are often 
20 invoked for the fiduciary exception to attorney-client privilege. Id. Some courts have 
21 held because ERISA trustees have a duty to disclose all the information regarding plan 
22 administration to their beneficiaries, the fiduciary exception acts as a competing legal 
23 principle to attorney-client privilege. Id. (citing Becher, 129 F.3d at 271-72). Other 
24 courts have reasoned that because the advice on plan administration is sought by the 
25 trustee on behalf of the plan's beneficiary, the beneficiary is the client for purposes of 
26 these communications and holder of the privilege. Id. (citing United States v. Evans, 796 
27 F.2d 264, 265-66 (9th Cir. 1986)). By this reasoning, the "fiduciary exception" is better 
2 the fiduciary seeks the advice for the benefit of the beneficiary, not when it seeks advice 
3 to protect or defend the fiduciary. Id. at 1063-64. 
4 To determine whether communications between a fiduciary and counsel are 
5 protected from disclosure to the beneficiary, courts "focus on the ‘intended recipient of 
6 the advice and the purpose for which it is sought,'" rather than on a distinction between 
7 fiduciary and non-fiduciary activities. Ely v. Bd. of Trustees of PACE Indus. Union-Mgmt. 
8 Pension Fund, 334 F.R.D. 289, 294–95 (D. Idaho 2020) (quoting Fischel v. Equitable Life 
9 Assurance, 191 F.R.D. 606, 609 (N.D. Cal. 2000)) ("[C]ourts should . . . focus on whether 
10 the advice was sought for the benefit of the beneficiaries, as opposed to whether the 
11 advice is sought for the trustee's own protection."). One court construed the Mett rule 
12 as "excluding from [the fiduciary exception] any advice whose goal is to advise the 
13 trustee about the legal implications of actions and decisions undertaken while 
14 performing its fiduciary obligations." Fischel, 191 F.R.D. at 609. This approach is 
15 preferred because: (1) it is sufficiently narrow to recognize that a fiduciary has a right to 
16 consult with counsel for its own protection, (2) when a fiduciary does so, the rational 
17 that the fiduciary is acting as the agent of the beneficiary no longer applies, (3) it 
18 protects the attorney-client privilege in close cases, and (4) it encourages fiduciaries to 
19 seek advice regarding the performance of their duties so that they are better informed 
20 and more readily willing to serve as fiduciaries. Mett, 178 F.3d at 1065. On the other 
21 hand, "the use of a fiduciary-nonfiduciary activity distinction as the touchstone for a 
22 privilege inquiry . . . is awkward at best, simply because the existence of a fiduciary duty 
23 may be an ultimate issue that simply cannot be resolved in the course of discovery 
24 disputes." Fischel, 191 F.R.D. at 609 (quoting Craig C. Martin & Matthew H. Metcalf, The 
25 Fiduciary Exception to the Attorney-Client Privilege, 34 Tort & Ins. L.J. 827 (1999)). 
26 Finally, regarding the attorney-client privilege, "hard cases should be resolved in 
27 favor of the privilege, not in favor of disclosure." Mett, 178 F.3d at 1065. This is 
2 (quoting Upjohn, 449 U.S. at 393). 
3 B. Analysis 
4 Because Ligand seeks to assert attorney-client privilege, it bears the burden of 
5 establishing the relationship and the privileged nature of the communications at-issue. 
6 Ligand has satisfied this burden by demonstrating that its communications with counsel 
7 at Latham & Watkins in January of 2018 were to seek advice about Ligand's exposure to 
8 liability in the face of competing demands regarding the QDRO. In their sworn 
9 declarations, John Ryan and Charles Berkman state that Ligand sought legal advice from 
10 outside litigation counsel regarding the litigation it expected from Plaintiff or Young, 
11 "depending on how the Ligand Defendants proceeded after receiving the QDRO." (ECF 
12 No. 202-10 at 3; ECF No. 202-11 at 3.) Additionally, both Berkman and Ryan declare 
13 they do not intend to reveal the substance of those communications or waive any 
14 privilege or protection. (ECF No. 202-10 at 2; ECF No. 202-11 at 2.) Furthermore, 
15 Plaintiff does not contest that the communications at-issue are protected by attorney-
16 client privilege, only whether the fiduciary exception renders them disclosable by 
17 Plaintiff. (ECF No. 202 at 18-22.) 
18 The critical question in determining whether the fiduciary exception applies is 
19 whether the legal advice was sought for the benefit of a plan beneficiary or for Ligand. 
20 Defendants have shown that Ligand's communications with counsel at Latham & 
21 Watkins regarding the QDRO were for the purpose of understanding Ligand's potential 
22 liabilities with respect to the various actions it might take. Both Ryan and Berkman 
23 declare that Ligand has sought advice from Latham & Watkins and Ryan since 2007 in 
24 multiple litigation-related matters. (ECF No. 202-10 at 2; ECF No. 202-11 at 2.) Ryan 
25 further declares that he communicates frequently with Berkman by telephone and 
26 email when "Ligand requires assistance with anticipated or ongoing litigation matters." 
27 (ECF No. 202-11 at 2, emphasis added.) Both declarations further state that beginning 
2 outside counsel, and threats of litigation. (ECF No. 202-11; ECF No. 202-10.) 
3 Importantly, Ryan explains that Berkman does not seek "advice on 401(k) plan 
4 administration issues," nor would he expect Berkman to do so because Ryan has "no 
5 expertise on 401(K) plan administrative matters." (ECF No. 202-11 at 2.) 
6 These declarations are consistent with the circumstances at the time of the 
7 communications. On January 5, 2018, after being notified of the QDRO, Plaintiff 
8 emailed Ligand with a list of problems he identified with the QDRO, telling Ligand what 
9 he believed it should do going forward—including communications it should have with 
10 Young's counsel and Fidelity, issues it should research, and how it ought to consult with 
11 Plaintiff before taking further action. (ECF No. 202-6 at 3-4.) Ligand's President and 
12 General Counsel replied to Plaintiff that the QDRO appeared to be valid, and Ligand 
13 would need to comply. (ECF No. 202-6 at 2-3.) Plaintiff responded, urging Ligand not to 
14 comply with the QDRO until he could consult an expert, later adding that it was 
15 common for plan administrators to take sixty days to respond to a QDRO order. (ECF 
16 No. 202-6 at 2.) Thus, at the outset, Ligand was subject to competing demands—from 
17 the Court that had issued the order, and from two competing beneficiaries seeking 
18 completely opposite outcomes. 
19 Ligand had reason to believe it was not permitted to follow Plaintiff's directives 
20 on the basis of his assertions about the legality of the QDRO. ERISA has established a 
21 checklist procedure for plan administrators to follow when qualifying a QDRO. See 
22 U.S.C. 29 §1056(d)(3)(C), (D). "[A] QDRO enquiry is relatively discrete, given the specific 
23 and objective criteria for a domestic relations order that qualifies as a QDRO . . . 
24 requirements that amount to a statutory checklist." Kennedy v. Plan Adm'r for DuPont 
25 Sav. & Inv. Plan, 555 U.S. 285, 301–02 (2009).3 Indeed, a plan "must pay the bearer of a 
26 

27 
2 of Dirs. Guild of Am.-Producer Pension Benefits Plans v. Tise, 234 F.3d 415, 424 (9th Cir. 
3 2000). Once Ligand had verified that the QDRO met the statutory requirements, Ligand 
4 had reason to be concerned about exposure to liability if it did not comply with the 
5 QDRO's terms and assign 100% of Plaintiff's total 401(k) account balance to Young as 
6 the designated Alternate Payee. (See ECF No. 202-5 at 3.) 
7 On January 8, 2018, Plaintiff continued to insist the QDRO was invalid. Plaintiff 
8 explained that the QDRO was trying to divide post-marital assets which was "illegal," 
9 and that there was no preceding motion or opportunity to object to the QDRO which 
10 the "law requires." (ECF No. 202-7 at 3.) Further, Plaintiff contended the QDRO was a 
11 "blanket request for funds," failing to consider what was still owed between Plaintiff 
12 and his ex-wife, and thus contrary to what the "law requires" in post-marital 
13 proceedings. (Id.) Plaintiff concluded his email stating he was going to hire a lawyer to 
14 quash the QDRO. (Id.) Ligand reached out to Latham & Watkins just two days later, on 
15 January 10, 2018. (ECF No. 202-9 at 5.) 
16 / / / 
17 

18 
19 (C) A domestic relations order meets the requirements of this subparagraph only if such order clearly 
 specifies- 
20 
 (i) the name and the last known mailing address (if any) of the participant and the name and mailing 
21 address of each alternate payee covered by the order, 
 (ii) the amount or percentage of the participant's benefits to be paid by the plan to each such alternate 
22 payee, or the manner in which such amount or percentage is to be determined, 
 (iii) the number of payments or period to which such order applies, and 
23 (iv) each plan to which such order applies. 
 (D) A domestic relations order meets the requirements of this subparagraph only if such order- 
24 
 (i) does not require a plan to provide any type or form of benefit, or any option, not otherwise 
25 provided under the plan, 
 (ii) does not require the plan to provide increased benefits (determined on the basis of actuarial value), 
26 and 
 (iii) does not require the payment of benefits to an alternate payee which are required to be paid to 
27 another alternate payee under another order previously determined to be a qualified domestic 
2 Plaintiff's first two arguments are interdependent, claiming that, (1) the email 
3 communications at-issue related to Ligand's administration of Plaintiff's 401(k) Plan, and 
4 (2) because Ligand had not yet made a final determination of the validity of the QDRO, 
5 Plaintiff was the 401(k) Plan's beneficiary and entitled to disclosure of the 
6 communications under the fiduciary exception. (ECF No. 202 at 19, 22.) But these 
7 claims ignore the holding in Mett that "where a fiduciary seeks legal advice for her own 
8 protection, the core purposes of the attorney-client privilege are seriously implicated 
9 and should trump the beneficiaries' general right to inspect documents." Mett, 178 
10 F.3d at 1065. 
11 Plaintiff, however, insists that "it is not until after the final determination that is, 
12 after the final administrative appeal that the interests of the Plan fiduciary and the 
13 beneficiary diverge for purposes of application of the fiduciary exception." (ECF No. 202 
14 at 20 (citing Stephan v. Unum Life Ins. Co. of Am., 697 F.3d 917, 931 (9th Cir. 2012)).) 
15 Plaintiff relies on Stephan to claim that the fiduciary exception should apply here 
16 because Ligand had not yet made a final determination of the QDRO's validity. But 
17 Stephan is factually distinguishable from this case because there were no competing 
18 interests in Stephan until a final decision was made, adverse to the beneficiary's wishes. 
19 In Stephan, the plaintiff appealed his insurance company's determination of his monthly 
20 disability benefit amount. 697 F.3d at 922. After the insurance company denied his 
21 appeal, the plaintiff sought disclosure of certain communications that occurred between 
22 the insurance company's claims analysts and in-house counsel regarding how they 
23 interpreted plaintiff's policy to reach the benefit determination. Id. at 932. The court 
24 held that the plaintiff was entitled to disclosure of the communications because at that 
25 time, the claims analysts' "goal was the determination of [the plaintiff's] disability 
26 earnings," and "advice on the amount of benefits [the plaintiff] was owed under the 
27 Plan . . . constitute[d] advice on plan administration." Id. at 933. But here, whether 
2 Ligand's handling of the QDRO. Thus, Ligand sought legal advice from Latham & 
3 Watkins to minimize its exposure in the face of the competing demands—not on behalf 
4 of Plaintiff or for purposes of Plaintiff's 401(k) Plan administration. As previously 
5 mentioned, Ryan declares that, "Mr. Berkman has not sought my advice on 401(K) plan 
6 administration issues, and I have no expertise on 401(K) plan administrative matters so I 
7 would not expect him to seek such advice from me." (ECF No. 202-11 at 2.) 
8 Plaintiff's third argument is that in order to preserve attorney-client privilege, 
9 "the Plan's fiduciaries must have requested legal advice to respond to a specific and 
10 imminent threat of litigation." (ECF No. 202 at 21 (citing Wit v United Behavioral Health, 
11 No. 14-2346, 2016 WL 258604, at *9 (N.D. Cal. Jan. 21, 2016)).) Plaintiff claims that he 
12 had not threatened Ligand with imminent litigation at the time of the communications, 
13 and that he sought to work with Ligand in resolving issues he saw with the QDRO rather 
14 than against it. (ECF No. 202 at 20-21.) Plaintiff's claims that he did not have adverse 
15 interests to Ligand are not persuasive. Ligand had an interest in complying with court 
16 orders directed at Ligand, and thereby avoiding contempt and other litigation based on 
17 any failure to comply. These interests were not shared by Plaintiff. Even though 
18 Plaintiff's requests were cloaked in the language of collaboration, Plaintiff repeatedly 
19 raised potential issues and duties he wanted Ligand to assume. (ECF No. 202-6; ECF No. 
20 202-7.) He also requested Ligand not comply with the order to permit Plaintiff time to 
21 investigate alternatives. (Id.) 
22 / / / 
23 / / / 
24 / / / 
25 / / / 
26 / / / 
27 / / / 
1 Ligand reasonably understood that "trouble was in the air." See Mett, 178 F.3d at 
2 || 1064 (finding indications that "[t]rouble was in the air" were reason enough to seek 
3 || legal advice for the purpose of potential liability). At the time Ligand sought counsel 
4 || regarding its liability, it was already subject to a specific court order, and Plaintiff's 
5 ||statements made it clear that two different people, Plaintiff and Young, claimed to be 
6 || beneficiaries under the 401(k) Plan. Ligand's reasons for seeking legal advice on its own 
7 || behalf were well-founded given Plaintiff's statements, Plaintiff's hiring outside counsel, 
8 || and Ligand's history of litigation involving Plaintiff and Young. 
9 IV. CONCLUSION 
10 For the reasons stated above, the Court finds the communications at-issue do not 
11 || fall under the fiduciary exception and are protected by attorney-client privilege. 
12 || Plaintiff's motion to compel further deposition testimony regarding Ligand's 
13 || communication with Latham & Watkins is DENIED. 
14 IT IS SO ORDERED. 
15 || Dated: November 15, 2023 _ a
